EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A human-caused fire began to burn Monday June 25, 2012 in the Scratchgravel hills in the West Central portion of the Helena Valley. Over 1,850 acres burned before the fire was brought under 100% containment. The fire caused numerous evacuations and destroyed a number of homes and trailers in the area as well as some outbuildings, a shed, and a car. There were no reports of injuries to residents in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A human-caused wildfire began to burn in the Scratchgravel hills of the West Central Helena Valley when a prescribed burn ember reignited causing a new fire to develop.

Multiple lightning strikes during the late evening hours of the 31st resulted in the start of several wildfires.  The East Sarpy, Little Dry Creek, Dawes, West Tullock Creek, Castle Rock II, and South Tullock Creek fires burned together on August 1st and 2nd and were re-named the Sarpy Hills Complex. ||The fire fuels were mainly grass and brush with some timber. Total area burned was 82,000 acres. There were about 400 personnel, 30 engines, eight crews, and many types of equipment used to fight the fire. The first fire originated on July 31st 6 miles northeast of Crow Agency. Total containment of the Sarpy Hills Complex was reached on August 9th. ||More detailed information can be found at www.inciweb.org.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific storm system interacted with a cold arctic airmass to bring widespread heavy snow to the forecast area between November 8 and 10, 2012. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow showers helped generate strong winds over portions of North-Central Montana. Wind gusts topped out in the 50 to 60 mph range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow showers passing over the region helped to generate strong wind gusts over portions of North-Central Montana.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong late-spring storm moved through the Northern Rocky Mountains bringing a mix of rain and snow to region. Higher elevations received the brunt of the snow but some lower elevations lost power and had down tree limbs. Station KXLH in Helena, MT was also briefly knocked off the air due to the winter weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 8 inches in 12 hours reported at the Divide SNOTEL (Elevation 7800 ft).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system over the northern plains, allowed very strong winds to be focused across northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds near and above 40 mph were common across the area with a maximum wind gust of 64 mph measured at the Malta South US-191 DOT site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry conditions and high temperatures resulted in the wildfire season beginning earlier than normal. June and July brought above normal temperatures and below normal precipitation. High temperatures during these months ranged from the upper 80s to lower 100s.||The Ash Creek Fire ignited on June 25th. It was caused by lightning and the origin was 10 miles east of Lame Deer. It burned in timber, juniper, pine, sage and grass. The fire burned 249,562 acres, 11 homes, and numerous outbuildings.  In addition, the wildfire destroyed a state highway communications tower and knocked out transmissions. The fire complex had devastating effects on livestock and grazing lands.||Four additional fires broke out within close proximity to and shortly after the Ash Creek Fire. These fires were the Taylor Creek, Horse Creek, Powerline and Coal Seam Fires. All five fires were combined into one large incident and renamed the Southeastern Montana Complex. Of the five fires in this complex, the Ash Creek Fire was the first to ignite. The complex was contained on July 10th.||The following acreage was consumed by each fire:|Ash Creek-249,562|Taylor Creek-62,134|Horse Creek-7,575|Powerline-5,265|Coal Seam-527|Total Complex acres-325,063||The cost of fighting this complex was $7.5 million. ||For additional information, please see the Incident Information website at|www.inciweb.org.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime to quarter sized hail was reported by the public and lasted 7 minutes about 2 miles northwest of Nashua in Valley County.

In addition, day time heating produced a very unstable atmosphere, which created an environment conducive to thunderstorms with heavy rain, hail, and strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In 8 minutes 0.25 inches of rain fell.

Low level moisture along with surface dew points in the 50s combined with a dry line/convergence zone helped contribute to the development of severe thunderstorms across portions of Southeast Montana. In addition, steep lapse rates and MLCAPE of 1000-1500 J/KG and 30-40 kt westerly mid level winds resulted in sufficient deep layer shear which helped a few organized severe thunderstorms to produce large hail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CO-OP observer reported quarter sized hail about 8 miles north of Brusett in northern Garfield County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sub-severe thunderstorms rolled through the area on June 13.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service employee reported mainly dime to pea sized hail, with a few stones penny sized about 2 miles south-southeast of Glasgow in Valley County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sub-severe thunderstorms rolled through the area on June 13.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel sized hail was reported 18 miles north of Poplar in Roosevelt County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms fired up in the afternoon and remained sub-severe in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported penny sized hail covering the ground 9 miles northwest of Froid in Roosevelt County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Convection fired in the morning of June 23 and produced a few severe reports near the Yellowstone River Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported quarter sized hail near the town of Glendive in Dawson County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous lightning strikes during the late evening hours on July 31st resulted in several wildfire starts. These wildfires were Jupiter, Big John, Beaver, Chalky, Sun Dance, and the Dawes fires. By the next day, August 1st, these fires were consolidated into one wildfire and re-named the Rosebud Complex. These fires were located 4 to 30 miles south of Rosebud, Montana. The fuels burned were Ponderosa pine, sage brush, and grass. The acreage burned was 171,444 acres which measured about 205 square miles. Three homes and two outbuildings were destroyed by the fire. The cost to fight the wildfire was $7.9 million. In addition, the wildfire destroyed a state highway communications tower and knocked out transmissions between dispatchers and troopers. The Rosebud Complex was fully contained by August 15th.||More detailed information can be found at www.inciweb.org.
A fast moving cold front pushed across western Montana. The tight surface pressure gradient associated with this feature produced strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were knocked down in the Upper Miller creek area, south of Missoula by strong non-thunderstorm winds. Power was out from about 4pm mdt until 6pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous cold front pushed across western Montana producing some very strong thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highway department reported several trees blown down across highway MT-83 near mile marker 48.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous cold front pushed across western Montana producing some very strong thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were blown down on the south side of Missoula. One tree was 10-12 inches in diameter and was snapped off at the base.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous cold front pushed across western Montana producing some very strong thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter four miles southeast of Creston estimated hail 1 inch in diameter, with hail 1 to 2 inches deep covering the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous cold front pushed across western Montana producing some very strong thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over 100 trees were blown down between 7 and 14 miles north of Seeley lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous cold front pushed across western Montana producing some very strong thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter one mile southeast of Marion estimated hail 1 inch in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous cold front pushed across western Montana producing some very strong thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail covered the ground about 1 inch deep. The hail was estimated at 0.75 inches in  diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Elevated convection pulsed up associated with low to mid tropospheric warming due to southwest flow. A weak shortwave trough was working its way through a broad, larger ridge over the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: No damage reported, but this is a very rural area with few trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Skibstad Wildfire started on the evening of July 25th and originated 8 miles southeast of Columbus near Highway 421 and grew to 3000 acres during the overnight hours. Officials believed the fire started when a fire in a building spread to the surrounding area. Stillwater County officials ordered evacuations for homes in the Shane Creek area south of the fire. However, no homes or buildings were destroyed as a result of the fire. Fire crews and 45 engines from Sweet Grass, Yellowstone, Stillwater and Carbon Counties along with state DNRC firefighters fought the blaze. Two helicopters and two air tankers were used in fighting the fire. The fuels for the fire were timber, grass and sage. Total personnel used to fight the fire was 234 and the total area burned was 3,354 acres. The fire was contained on July 29th around 8 PM.

Elevated convection pulsed up associated with low to mid tropospheric warming due to southwest flow. A weak shortwave trough was working its way through a broad, larger ridge over the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Initial reports came from Richland County Sheriff's Department and then also from trained spotters in the area.  There were a total of 8 large steel tanks blown over that weigh 12,000-14,000 pounds each. They were empty, and not staked into the ground yet.  They stood about 25 feet tall and 8 feet across.  Consulted with expert damage assessors with NWS Warning Decision Training Branch and at the University of Oklahoma, who agreed with the NWS Glasgow wind speed estimate of 95 mph winds to blow those tanks over.  A 5th wheel camper was also blown over at the site, and an occupant was injured, but was not transported to the closest medical center.  A pickup truck was also crushed under the tanks.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six power poles and lines were downed in this area.  Wind speeds estimated on other damage in the area and the EF Scale Toolkit.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported mainly dime sized hail but some of the hail was half dollar sized 3 miles east-northeast of Cohagen in Garfield County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm southwest flow created unstable snow conditions at the higher elevations and some freezing drizzle in the valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several small auto accidents occurred shortly after 7 am due to light freezing rain. A charter bus rolled and ejected passengers around 7:16 am on I-90 east of Missoula. I-90 was closed in both directions due to the accident. There were 2 deaths confirmed and 31 injuries.

In addition, day time heating produced a very unstable atmosphere, which created an environment conducive to thunderstorms with heavy rain, hail, and strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public estimated 1 inch hail 7 miles northeast of Corvallis.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist Pacific system moved into western Montana and north central Idaho, and dropped some significant snowfall totals mainly at the higher elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall between 3 pm and 7 pm was measured at 6 inches, 4 miles east northeast of Blacktail at Marias pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front with a very tight surface pressure gradient moved into western Montana producing strong damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A gust of wind was measured by the ASOS at the Missoula airport of 59 mph.  Also, strong winds uprooted three mature trees two miles east southeast of Hamilton. There is also one large pine tree leaning. A storage shed was blown about 100 feet and destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Dahl Fire was a lightning caused fire and was first reported at 1245pm on Tuesday, June 26th. The fire was fueled by high temperatures, low relative humidities and gusty winds. The fuels consisted of grass, sage and timber (ponderosa pine). The fire burned approximately 22,045 acres. Seventy three homes and hundreds of other structures were destroyed in the fast-moving blaze. The fire was contained on July 3rd. The cost in fighting the fire was roughly $2,550,000.00. For more information, please see www.inciweb.org.

A vigorous Pacific storm system moved into western Montana, with an associated strong upper jet. Large scale lift and a surface cold front caused widespread strong surface winds and significant precipitation totals.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were multiple reports of trees down on power lines in and around Columbia Falls. The power went out at 1130 am and was still out at 1 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific storm system interacted with a cold arctic airmass to bring widespread heavy snow to the forecast area between November 8 and 10, 2012. Impacts from this storm included traffic accidents, road/school closures and aviation delays. Towards the end of the storm, strong winds developed and caused blizzard conditions for areas along the Rocky Mountain Front and central Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coop Observer 1 mile NW of Heart Butte measures 24-hour snowfall of 17.0 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong wind gusts were observed in the canyons along I-90, with white caps on the Clark fork river 1 mile south southwest of Bearmouth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable northwest flow aloft brought heavy rain and snow showers to Eastern Big Horn and South Central Rosebud Counties. In addition, 3 to 5 inches of snow fell over the higher hills. The rain combined with the melting of snow on frozen ground resulted in minor flooding of low lying areas during the afternoon of the 22nd through the morning hours of the 23rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County officials reported that minor flooding occurred across portions of Southern Rosebud County. Up to an inch of rain had fallen along with several inches of snow over the higher hills. The rain falling on frozen ground, combined with snow melt, resulted in minor flooding of low lying areas.  Several ice jams were reported on Rosebud, Lame Deer and Muddy Creeks resulting in water over the roads in some locations, as well as the main road in the town of Birney. In addition, an ice jam was reported on the Tongue River and Hanging Woman Creek north and west of Birney. One rancher had to rescue calves from a flooded area. Another rancher had a chicken coup flooded killing 4 hens, as well as a stranded horse.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable northwest flow aloft brought heavy rain and snow showers to Eastern Big Horn and South Central Rosebud Counties. In addition, 3 to 5 inches of snow fell over the higher hills. The rain combined with the melting of snow on frozen ground resulted in minor flooding of low lying areas during the afternoon of the 22nd through the morning hours of the 23rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County officials reported that minor flooding occurred across Eastern Big Horn County. Up to an inch of rain had fallen along with several inches of snow in the higher hills. The rain falling on frozen ground, combined with snow melt, resulted in minor flooding of low lying areas. Lodge Grass Creek flooded some fields. In addition, an ice jam along the Little Big Horn River south of Highway 212 at the Second Bridge Area resulted in water backing up over roads in a few locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Seven snowmobilers departed Cooke City, Montana and ascended Miller Creek drainage via Daisy Pass Trail Road. Approximately 2.25 miles up the Road the group stopped to play in a small meadow downhill from the road. At the time of the avalanche, the victim was attempting to turn his snowmobile around but got stuck on the steep cut bank on the uphill side of the road putting he and three other riders in the path of the avalanche. The victim was completely buried; the three others were partially buried. The group started a beacon search, located the victim within two minutes and had him completely dug out in ten minutes. The victim did not have a pulse and did not survive. It is unclear whether the avalanche released naturally or was triggered by the victim. The slide ran approximately 600 vertical feet and left a debris pile 4-6 feet deep. More detailed information can be found at www.mtavalanche.com.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Please see episode narrative.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface low pressure moving across southern Canada contributed to a tight gradient resulting in high winds across most of North Central and Southwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 58 mph was reported at Townsend 8W (the Elkhorn RAWS site). Many trees were reported down in the Townsend area. Power was also reported out in Townsend due to a tree falling on a power pole. Many ice houses were blown over on area lakes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure moving across Southern Canada resulted in a tight pressure gradient and high winds across portions of the Billings Forecast Area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 61 mph was reported in Fort Smith.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surface low across Eastern Montana and Western North Dakota produced strong winds across portions of the Billings Forecast Area. In addition, an unusual thunderstorm event also resulted in strong wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Some limbs were broken off trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surface low across Eastern Montana and Western North Dakota produced strong winds across portions of the Billings Forecast Area. In addition, an unusual thunderstorm event also resulted in strong wind gusts.

A strong surface low across Eastern Montana and Western North Dakota produced strong winds across portions of the Billings Forecast Area. In addition, an unusual thunderstorm event also resulted in strong wind gusts.

A deep and extensive cold mass of frigid arctic air spread over the northern high plains while a significant plume of pacific moisture streamed over the northern Rockies - aided by a strong 170-190 knot upper-level jet stream.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The greatest wind chill impacts across the area were noted through the early morning hours with a minimum wind chill reading of -42 degrees recorded at the Medicine Lake RAWS site between 5:58 AM and 6:58 AM MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Over a period of 5 days, a large, complex and dangerous storm system moved into Idaho and western Montana. This storm had a number of factors that interacted to produce a wide range of snowfall totals, precipitation types and temperature ranges. First, a very cold arctic air mass moved out of Canada into eastern Montana and then filtered across the divide into western Montana. Second, a large Pacific storm moved in from the west, this system had an abundant moisture feed, as well as jet stream support. Hazardous travel caused school closures in the Missoula area for two consecutive days, a very rare occurrence.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall was reported 6 miles southeast of Clinton, where 13 inches of snow was measured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving across Southern Canada combined with surface high pressure across Eastern Idaho resulted in very windy conditions across the Livingston area and Eastern Foothills of the Beartooth/Absaroka Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 50 to 55 mph with gusts to 76 mph were recorded at the Livingston Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm system brought heavy snow to the Northern Rocky Mountain Front and portions of Gallatin County. Snow amounts of 8 to 10 inches were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ten inches of snow fell at West Yellowstone 5S. Also, eight inches of snow fell at West Yellowstone 10S and eight inches of snow fell at West Yellowstone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Over a period of 5 days, a large, complex and dangerous storm system moved into Idaho and western Montana. This storm had a number of factors that interacted to produce a wide range of snowfall totals, precipitation types and temperature ranges. First, a very cold arctic air mass moved out of Canada into eastern Montana and then filtered across the divide into western Montana. Second, a large Pacific storm moved in from the west, this system had an abundant moisture feed, as well as jet stream support. Hazardous travel caused school closures in the Missoula area for two consecutive days, a very rare occurrence.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain was reported 3 miles east of Florence, forming a crust on the snow and the ground. Roads were slick.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure east of the Divide combined with high pressure over Yellowstone Park resulted in very strong winds across the Upper Yellowstone and Upper Stillwater River Valleys and adjacent plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 50-61 mph with gusts up to 81 mph occurred across the Livingston area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong westerly flow aloft contributed to high winds at the surface over North Central and portions of Southwest Montana. Wind gusts to 87 mph were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 61 mph was reported at Sunburst 6NNW (the Sweet Grass DOT site).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong westerly flow aloft contributed to overnight high winds along the Rocky Mountain Front. Wind gusts in excess of 60 mph were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 64 mph was reported at the Heart Butte RAWS site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep and extensive cold mass of frigid arctic air spread over the northern high plains while a significant plume of pacific moisture streamed over the northern Rockies - aided by a strong 170-190 knot upper-level jet stream.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: For the late overnight hours previous to this, wind chills between -30 and -38 were common across the area.  Sometime between 2:44 AM and 3:44 AM MST, this RAWS site at Bluff Creek recorded a wind chill of -40 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep and extensive cold mass of frigid arctic air spread over the northern high plains while a significant plume of pacific moisture streamed over the northern Rockies - aided by a strong 170-190 knot upper-level jet stream.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The greatest wind chill impacts across the area were noted through the early morning hours with a minimum wind chill reading of -46 degrees recorded at the King Coulee RAWS site between 5:44 AM and 6:44 AM MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong westerly flow aloft contributed to overnight high winds along the Rocky Mountain Front. Wind gusts in excess of 60 mph were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 63 mph was reported at Pendroy 17W (the Theodore Roosevelt Memorial Ranch).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An impressive winter storm system moved into north central Idaho and western Montana on the 22nd and continued to effect the region through the 24th. Initially a steady stream of Pacific moisture moved in from the west. This helped produce areas of heavy snowfall over the mountains and passes. The valleys received rain. As the main snowfall was ending a tight surface pressure gradient and a strong jet stream produced very strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ski resort at Lookout pass reported 14 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An impressive winter storm system moved into north central Idaho and western Montana on the 22nd and continued to effect the region through the 24th. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree and billboard were blown down in Superior along with some shingle damage to the roof of an elementary school.  Nearly 100 trees were reported to be blown down, some with their root balls attached and some snapped in half. Nine structures were damaged from falling trees, along with three homes and three moble homes. According to the fire chief, there were no injuries. The total swath of damage was estimated to be 6 miles long, from Superior east to mile marker 52 on I-90.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The East Fork Fire was reported on August 22, 2012 and was caused by lightning from thunderstorms that went through the Lincoln area on Tuesday, August 21st. The fire burned over 1,100 acres in mostly heavy timber of the Scapegoat Wilderness in the Helena National Forest-Lincoln Ranger District. The fire burned along the border of Powell and Lewis and Clark counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low level moisture along with surface dew points in the 50s combined with a dry line/convergence zone helped contribute to the development of severe thunderstorms across portions of Southeast Montana. In addition, steep lapse rates and MLCAPE of 1000-1500 J/KG and 30-40 kt westerly mid level winds resulted in sufficient deep layer shear which helped a few organized severe thunderstorms to produce large hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Delphia Wildfire was reported on August 24th at approximately 2 pm. It was caused by a lightning strike on the evening of August 23rd and originated 16 miles east/northeast of Roundup. The lightning ignited fire remained dormant until the afternoon of the 24th when hot temperatures, low afternoon humidities and windy conditions resulted in the fire exploding to 18,000 acres and then again to as much as 30,000 acres on the 26th. Total area burned was 40,653 acres. ||Air tankers and helicopters helped in the fire effort. The wildfire grew to almost 55 square miles. About 15 to 20 outbuildings were destroyed, but no homes were lost. The area from Musselshell to Melstone and south to Custer lost power when the fire destroyed a 50-kilovolt transmission line. The fire burned in timber, grass and sage. Almost 370 crew members fought the fire including five helicopters, three heavy air tankers, 48 engines, 14 tenders, seven dozers, four Type 2 initial attack crews, two Type 2 fire crews and two hot shot crews. ||The fire was contained on August 31st at approximately 12 am. More detailed information can be found at www.inciweb.org.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Rosebud Wildfire originated 10 miles southwest of Roscoe, Montana on Tuesday, August 28th, at approximately 1:08 pm. The fire burned 2,397 acres and was 100% contained on Saturday, September 1st at arroximately 5 pm. The fuels consisted of grass and understory pine and grass with intermixed aspen. ||Mandatory evacuations were ordered for approximately 20 residences along Getaway Trail five miles south of Roscoe. Although one outbuilding was reported to have burned, no houses were destroyed. Total personnel consisted of about 195 people, 30 engines, a pair each of bulldozers and water tenders, three helicopters, one heavy air tanker and two small engine tankers. The cause of the wildfire remains under investigation. More detailed information can be found at www.inciweb.org.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Dugan fire was a wildfire that originated 2 miles south of Ekalaka in Carter County  on Friday, September 14th at approximately 10:00 pm. At this time, the cause of the fire remains unknown. The wildfire fuels involved were grass, shrubs and Ponderosa Pine timber in rough, steep coulees. The fire burned 10,675 acres and was 100% contained on Wednesday, September 19th at approximately 6:00 pm. The cause of the fire at this time remains unknown and is under investigation. The fire destroyed an estimated three outbuildings and an uninhabited trailer but no homes. However, the fire did burn the Ekalaka Park Campground, destroying a handful of picnic tables. About 200 people were assigned to the fire, along with 25 engines, three helicopters, three graders and a pair of dozers. More detailed information can be found at www.inciweb.org.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple lightning strikes during the late evening hours of July 31st resulted in the start of several wildfires. The East Sarpy, Little Dry Creek, Dawes, West Tullock Creek, Castle Rock II, and South Tullock Creek fires burned together on August 1st and 2nd and were re-named the Sarpy Hills Complex.||The fire fuels were mainly grass and brush with some timber. Total area burned was 82,000 acres. There were about 400 personnel, 30 engines, eight crews, and many types of equipment used to fight the fire. The first fire originated on July 31st 6 miles northeast of Crow Agency. Total containment of the Sarpy Hills Complex was reached on August 9th.||More detailed information can be found at www.inciweb.org.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Highway 87 Wildfire was reported around 3:00 pm on the afternoon of Monday, August 20th. The wildfire was located approximately 6 miles southeast of Billings. The fire quickly grew to several hundred acres and was fueled by tinder-dry grass and timber, along with hot, dry, windy conditions. Aggressive ground and aerial attack held the fire to 1000 to around 2000 acres during the afternoon and evening of August 20th. One structure was confirmed lost. However, no injuries were reported. The fire was human caused and, at this time, remains under investigation. The fire grew to about 1,933 acres and was contained at approximately 6:00 pm on August 25th. More detailed information can be found at www.inciweb.org.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Pine Creek Wildfire originated 9 miles south of Livingston, Montana on August 29th at approximately 3 pm. The fire started near the Yellowstone River and quickly burned through the town of Pine Creek. After which, it moved into the steep, inaccessible terrain of the Absaroka-Beartooth Wilderness in the Gallatin National Forecast. Local firefighters, helicopters, and retardant airtankers were able to save many homes, the Pine Creek Church, and the Pine Creek Cafe. However, five homes and several outbuildings were lost during the first day of the fire. On the second day of the fire, the fire threatened more homes as it moved south onto private land.||The fuels burned were Timber (liter and understory), heavy, dead standing and down, as well as gray-stage beetle kill trees. The size of the fire was 8,572 acres and will more than likely be revised. The wildfire was contained on October 18th. The cause of the fire is human and remains under investigation. More detailed information can be found at www.inciweb.org.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Wilson Fire was reported on Saturday, September 22nd at 2pm and originated 8 miles southeast of Roundup, Montana. The fuels burned were timber and sage. Musselshell County and other fire departments responded to the blaze, and Montana DNRC assisted with air tankers, helicopters and engines. The fire, believed to be human caused, quickly grew in tinder-dry fuels to around 4100 acres. At one point, U.S. Highway 87 just south of Roundup was closed for a time being. About 50 homes were ordered to evacuate but there were no reports of primary structures destroyed. A 150-man firefighting crew, composed of members from small, regional fire departments and the state Department of Natural Resources and Conservation fought the blaze. In addition, three helicopters dropped water buckets and two fire-retardant bombers dropped a red chemical blanket along the fire's edge. More detailed information can be found at www.inciweb.org.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Stonewall Fire was ignited by lightning from a thunderstorm that came through the area at about 3 p.m. on Monday, August 27. The fire burned about 12 acres in heavy, dense timber in the Helena National Forest-Lincoln Ranger District, about one mile south of the Stonewall Lookout and about five miles north of the town of Lincoln, Montana. Smoke was visible from Highway 200. The fire may continue to smolder for a period of time but is considered 100% contained.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Stonewall Fire was ignited by lightning from a thunderstorm that came through the area at about 3 p.m. on Monday, August 27. The fire burned about 12 acres in heavy, dense timber in the Helena National Forest-Lincoln Ranger District, about one mile south of the Stonewall Lookout and about five miles north of the town of Lincoln, Montana. Smoke was visible from Highway 200. The fire may continue to smolder for a period of time but is considered 100% contained.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Elbow Pass Complex is comprised of five different lightning ignitions from July 12 through July 31. These fires have merged into one fire, the Elbow Pass Complex: Triple Divide Fire, Rapid Creek Fire, Elbow Pass Fire, and Bar Creek Fire. The fires are generally located 25 miles west southwest of Augusta, Montana. The Falls Point fire is south of the Complex. All are located in inaccessible terrain in heavy fuels. The fires are in the Bob Marshall and Scapegoat Wilderness Areas and are on three forests; The Flathead, Lolo and Lewis & Clark. As of September 6, 2012, the fire has burned 21,069 acres.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Elbow Pass Complex is a wildfire group comprised of five different lightning ignitions from July 12 through July 31, 2012.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the region.  Instability and dry air lead to a widespread strong wind event as the cold front moved through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fort Peck Dam observation site measured sustained winds of 43 mph with a gust to 58 mph.  Damage did occur in nearby Valley County with the passage of this cold front through the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the region.  Instability and dry air lead to a widespread strong wind event as the cold front moved through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS employee called to report winds gusts over 60 mph. The Glasgow ASOS had a 62 mph wind gust, and a weather station near Fort Peck had a gust to 65 mph.  There were several reports from the public as well of tree branches broken off, a shed was flipped over, and a 5th wheel camper was rolled several times in the Park Grove area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushing through the region produced some severe thunderstorms that caused some damage as it moved eastward towards the North Dakota border. The most significant damage was in Richland County from Highway 201 from Girard eastward to Highway 16.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six power poles and lines were snapped and damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushing through the region produced some severe thunderstorms that caused some damage as it moved eastward towards the North Dakota border. The most significant damage was in Richland County from Highway 201 from Girard eastward to Highway 16.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of 21 wooden power poles over a 2.1 mile stretch were snapped off at the bases with an additional two leaning enough to need replacement.  A 4000 bushel grain bin was tipped over and an open steel shed had the roof blown off and thrown 150 yards to the north.  The measured wind gust was from a Richland County agrimet station right in the area of the poles that were down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and unstable atmosphere became favorable for the development of linear thunderstorms across northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter estimated quarter-sized hail stones.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large Pacific trough of low pressure approached western Montana in early June, setting up a moist southwest flow over the region. In addition, day time heating produced a very unstable atmosphere, which created an environment conducive to thunderstorms with heavy rain, hail, and strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was estimated at 1 inch one mile west southwest of Georgetown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Elevated convection pulsed up associated with low to mid tropospheric warming due to southwest flow. A weak shortwave trough was working its way through a broad, larger ridge over the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail and heavy rain was reported by the public 11 miles northwest of Hinsdale in Valley County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous Pacific storm system moved into western Montana, with an associated strong upper jet. Large scale lift and a surface cold front caused widespread strong surface winds and significant precipitation totals.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The rainfall total for 24 hours was 2.10 inches.  This was measured 13 miles north northeast of Heron.
